evals.prompt: I'm reviewing a patch on pgsql-hackers that adds a new GUC plus a new inline helper function in src/include/utils/. What should I check?
evals.with_skill:
Run the seven-phase review.

Phase 1 (Submission, 5 min): unified/context diff with context lines, vN-0001-*.patch, applies cleanly to current master (`git checkout master && git pull && git am`), test under src/test/ (a regress test that SET/SHOWs the new GUC), doc update under doc/src/sgml/config.sgml. If any miss → Waiting on Author before going deeper.

Phase 2 (Usability): cover-email goal matches diff. GUC name consistent with neighboring GUCs (snake_case, sensible category, unit/list flags right). Sensible interactions with parallel query, replication. Is src/include/utils/ the right home for the inline helper or should it live next to its users?

Phase 3 (Feature test): -Dcassert=true -Ddebug=true build, `ninja && meson test`. No new warnings. New tests fail when you revert just the code part. Edges: postgresql.conf, ALTER SYSTEM, SET LOCAL, SET inside subtransaction with rollback, SIGHUP reload, invalid values, NULL.

Phase 4 (Performance): inline helper on a hot path? pgbench baseline vs patched. Hot-loop micro-optimization → test at larger N to flush out hidden quadratic. Per-row palloc → short-lived MemoryContext (hand off to `memory-contexts` skill if uncertain).

Phase 5 (Coding): style matches surrounding module, no long lines, comments explain WHY, uses ereport/palloc/MemoryContext/ResourceOwner, error messages style-correct (delegate to `error-handling` and `coding-style` skills for the detailed rules), no -Wall -Wextra warnings.

Phase 6 (Architecture): inline functions and macros in src/include/ are part of the extension-visible ABI — same back-branch caution as struct layout (new members at end, no signature changes). Locking order consistent (hand off to `locking` skill). No catalog change implied here, but if any → CATALOG_VERSION_NO bump + OID assignment + master-only (see `catalog-conventions`). Does the GUC foreclose extensions (might it need to become a list later)?

Phase 7 (Review review): blocking vs nits clearly separated; asks concrete ("please add a test for SET LOCAL inside a subxact that rolls back" beats "more tests"). Plain-text inline reply on pgsql-hackers, preserve threading, flip CF entry to Waiting on Author / Ready for Committer.

evals.prompt: A CommitFest patch touches WAL and adds a new XLOG record type for a new index AM. What's my review checklist for it?
evals.with_skill:
Seven phases, with Phase 6 carrying the weight and a handoff to the `wal-and-xlog` deep skill.

Phase 1 (Submission): vN-0001-*.patch, applies to master, crash-recovery TAP test under src/test/recovery/ present, docs updated for the new AM.

Phase 2 (Usability): cover-letter claim matches diff. AM registered correctly in pg_am. Logical replication: does the new record need a decode callback or is it irrelevant? Physical replication / parallel query interactions thought through.

Phase 3 (Feature test): assertion build mandatory. `meson test` (regress + isolation + recovery). Crash-recovery: build state, SIGKILL postmaster, restart, verify recovery replays. Revert the redo function — new TAP test must fail. Verify pg_waldump output via rm_desc/rm_identify. (`testing` skill for picking the right test flavor.)

Phase 4 (Performance): WAL volume regression on existing workloads? Per-record allocation? FPI rules with XLogRegisterBuffer / REGBUF_* flags?

Phase 5 (Coding): redo uses XLogReadBufferForRedo idiom, no palloc inside critical section, START_CRIT_SECTION/END_CRIT_SECTION correct, error messages style-correct, no new warnings. (`error-handling` + `memory-contexts` skills.)

Phase 6 (Architecture — deep, hand off to `wal-and-xlog`):
  - XLOG_PAGE_MAGIC bumped — mandatory for any WAL format change.
  - New RMID in src/include/access/rmgrlist.h with rm_redo / rm_desc / rm_identify / rm_startup / rm_cleanup / rm_mask.
  - rm_desc and rm_identify so pg_waldump works.
  - Master-only, never backpatchable.
  - RM ID range: built-in needs a reserved ID; out-of-tree AM uses RM_EXPERIMENTAL_ID.
  - Locking order in redo matches the normal write path (hand off to `locking` skill) — out-of-order = recovery deadlock.
  - Hint-bit handling: MarkBufferDirtyHint vs MarkBufferDirty — wrong choice corrupts recovery.
  - Buffer pin / content-lock interactions preserved.
  - On-disk format change → pg_upgrade path or version-gate.
  - New pg_am row → CATALOG_VERSION_NO bump + OID (see `catalog-conventions`).

Phase 7: blocking vs nits, concrete asks, plain-text reply, flip CF state.

evals.prompt: Pre-submission review: my own patch claims a 30% speedup on hash join probes by reordering a hot loop. How do I verify the claim before sending to hackers?
evals.with_skill:
Pre-submission self-review per the seven phases, ruthlessly, with Phase 4 as the focus and `patch-submission` + `commit-message-style` skills as companions for the final hand-off.

Phase 1: patch applies clean to current master, vN-0001-*.patch, tests present, doc/release-note line if user-visible.

Phase 2: claim in cover letter must match diff exactly (workload, query shape, numbers). Write a precise repro recipe.

Phase 3: -Dcassert=false -Ddebug=false (release-equivalent) for benchmarking; NEVER benchmark with cassert. Separately, run check-world on the assertion build.

Phase 4 (focus):
  - Reproduce on the same hardware, multiple runs (median of >=3, ideally pgbench -T 60 -j N -c M).
  - Master vs patched, identical flags and data.
  - Regression hunt on unrelated workloads: pgbench tpc-b + representative joins the patch didn't target.
  - Memory: did you trade CPU for memory? Watch RSS.
  - Hot-loop micro-optimization → test at significantly larger N than your own benchmark; a faster constant factor that conceals a quadratic term is a footgun.
  - Microbenchmark vs end-to-end: a 30% hot-loop speedup may be 2% end-to-end. Measure both, be honest in the cover letter.

Phase 5: readability not sacrificed for perf. Comments explain WHY (cache locality, branch prediction, fewer allocations). No leftover debug elog. `git diff --check` clean. (`coding-style` skill.)

Phase 6: any data-structure layout change → ABI / catversion / WAL-magic implications. Inline functions added to src/include/ are part of the ABI surface.

Phase 7 (self-review): tests cover corner cases; commit history split into logical units (refactor first, optimization second); no debug elog; no commented-out code; catversion / WAL magic / control version bumps where needed.

Posting: small table — master tps, patched tps, run count, hardware, build flags, exact repro recipe. Numerical claims without a recipe get bounced. (`commit-message-style` for the trailer block before `git format-patch`.)
